settlement_intro_variants: The value of a {accident_lower} case at {airport} depends on four things: how severe the injury is, how strong the evidence is, how many defendants can be named, and which state the claim is filed in. Cases at {iata} range from {settlement_range} — but those numbers assume a single defendant. When {op}, {food_op}, and {park_op} are all named, total available insurance coverage is significantly higher.

Most victims of {accident_lower} incidents at {airport} dramatically underestimate what their case is worth. The number you see — {settlement_range} — is the range for cases with moderate evidence and a single defendant. Cases with strong CCTV evidence, clear liability, and multiple defendants named often settle above the top of that range.

Settlement values in {accident_lower} cases at {airport} are driven by the same factors courts use to value any personal injury claim — medical expenses, lost wages, pain and suffering — plus airport-specific multipliers that apply when {op} is the defendant: prior incident history, evidence of inadequate maintenance, and the number of contractors sharing liability.

{accident_lower} cases at {airport} settle across a wide range because no two injuries are identical and no two airports have the same liability structure. What your case is worth depends specifically on your injury severity, the zone where it occurred, which operator controlled that zone, and whether prior incidents at that location can be documented.

The {settlement_range} figure for {accident_lower} claims at {airport} is an average across all severity levels. Victims with serious injuries — fractures, spinal damage, traumatic brain injury — typically recover at or above the upper end of that range. Victims with minor injuries who have strong evidence and multiple named defendants often exceed the average as well.

Your {accident_lower} case at {airport} is not worth the first number the insurance company offers. {op}'s claims team starts low deliberately. The actual value of your case — based on your injuries, the evidence available, and the full defendant roster including {food_op} and {park_op} — is typically 3-5x what the initial offer suggests.

How much your {accident_lower} case at {iata} is worth depends on decisions made in the first 72 hours after the incident. Strong CCTV footage, a preserved maintenance log showing the hazard existed before your arrival, and prior incident records at the same location can double or triple the settlement value of an otherwise standard claim.

The settlement range for {accident_lower} cases at {airport} — {settlement_range} — reflects cases where victims had adequate representation, preserved evidence early, and named all liable parties. Victims who accepted the first offer from {op}'s insurance adjuster typically recovered a fraction of that range.
